family.name: K-pop
family.description: Section-aware K-pop harmony with bright hooks, dark drops, and emotional ballad contrast.

substyles.name: K-pop Bright Easy-Listening
substyles.description: Polished major-key K-pop with clean hook loops, clear lift, and easy-listening payoff.

substyles.name: K-pop Dark Synth / Futuristic
substyles.description: Darker synth-led K-pop with dramatic loop pressure, drop framing, and cinematic contrast.

substyles.name: K-pop Ballad / Emotional
substyles.description: Emotional K-pop ballad writing with slower turns, stronger release, and borrowed-color moments.

explanationTemplates.content: This {sectionIntent} stays bright by keeping the hook readable while the cadence leans {cadenceType} instead of overcomplicating the loop.
In {sectionIntent}, {substyleName} usually increases lift through cleaner dominant pull and a brighter payoff slot.
Notice how {rhythmName} keeps the progression producer-friendly: the harmony moves enough to feel polished, but the loop still leaves topline space.
This {sectionIntent} works because the dark loop identity stays intact while {specialMoveNames} can sharpen the drop or build without changing the core root path.
For {sectionIntent}, {substyleName} leans on pressure and contrast more than sweetness, so the progression should feel like it is tightening or reframing.
The dark-synth trick is keeping {cadenceType} energy inside a repeatable loop. That lets the production feel heavy without turning the harmony into noise.
This {sectionIntent} feels emotional because {substyleName} keeps the release gentle, then uses richer color only where the phrase can actually carry it.
In {sectionIntent}, the job is not speed. The job is direction: make the next arrival feel inevitable through cadence pressure or borrowed color.
When {rhythmName} slows the harmony down, every color chord matters more. That is why this substyle can support richer voicings without losing clarity.
